params.text:
Privacy is more than a closed door

When people think about privacy, they often picture a curtain drawn during personal care. That physical privacy matters, but the people you support also have a right to information privacy — control over who knows what about their lives, health and history. Every detail you learn on shift, from a diagnosis to a family worry, belongs to the person, not to you.

  • Confidentiality: keeping personal information private and sharing it only when there is a proper reason.
  • Data protection: the UK GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018 require records to be kept accurately, securely and no longer than needed.

Handling information the right way

Confidentiality is not secrecy. You share what you must to keep someone safe and well, and no more. A useful test is the need-to-know principle: does this colleague actually need this detail to do their job?

  • Ask before you share: seek consent where you can, and explain who will see the information.
  • Keep records safe: lock away paper notes, log out of care systems, and never discuss people in lifts, corridors or on social media.
  • Know the limits: if someone is at risk of harm, you may need to pass information on to keep them safe, and you should tell your manager.

params.question: A relative you have never met phones the office and asks how their father slept last night. What is the safest response?

answers.text: Explain you cannot share information without knowing they are entitled to it, and check with your manager first.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Verifying identity and the person's own wishes about sharing protects their confidentiality.

answers.text: Give a brief update, since it is only a family member asking.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Not safe. You cannot confirm the caller's identity or whether the person wants information shared with them.

answers.text: Refuse all contact and hang up without explanation.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: That goes too far. You can stay polite and helpful while still protecting confidentiality.

answers.text: Ask another resident who knows the family whether it is fine to share.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Discussing someone's care with an unrelated person is itself a breach of confidentiality.
